title: Transition-state lattice modes and the breakdown of adiabatic tunneling for hydrogen and deuterium in bcc Nb
authors: P. Graham Pritchard, James M. Rondinelli
abstract: Interstitial hydrogen and deuterium in body-centered-cubic metals constitute archetypal quantum tunneling systems. Their relevance has been renewed by the connection between hydrogenic tunneling in Nb and defect-induced decoherence in superconducting qubits, motivating a predictive microscopic theory. Existing theoretical treatments invoke an adiabatic separation between the light interstitial and the host lattice, an assumption whose validity has not been rigorously established for hydrogenic species. Here, we show that the experimentally measured tunnel splittings of O-trapped H and D in bcc Nb are quantitatively reproduced only within a five-dimensional (5D) Lattice-Renormalized Born-Oppenheimer (LRBO) framework. This approach treats three interstitial modes and two judiciously selected lattice modes, which includes a transition-state mode, on equal quantum footing. By recasting nested Born-Oppenheimer hierarchies within this same formalism and benchmarking against modern \textit{ab initio} potential energy surfaces, we show that adiabatic separation of the light particle from lattice dynamics is satisfied only in the positive-muon ($μ^{+}$) mass limit. In contrast, tunneling for H and D is fundamentally a collective, nonadiabatic process mediated by anharmonic lattice couplings. Finally, we show that the breakdown of adiabaticity can be anticipated from simple energy estimates involving the ground-state light-particle energy evaluated at a small number of fixed lattice configurations, providing a practical criterion for assessing the validity of adiabatic tunneling theories in other systems.
summary: This paper proves that the standard adiabatic approximation fails for hydrogen and deuterium tunneling in Niobium, showing instead that the process is a nonadiabatic, collective interaction with the lattice. This finding is crucial for understanding how interstitial defects contribute to decoherence in superconducting quantum circuits.
main_problem: Investigating the validity of the adiabatic approximation (separation of light-particle and lattice dynamics) for hydrogen and deuterium tunneling in bcc Niobium.
main_result: The study demonstrates that adiabatic separation is only valid for extremely light particles like muons, whereas H and D tunneling is a fundamentally nonadiabatic, collective process mediated by anharmonic lattice couplings.
method: The authors use a 5D Lattice-Renostormalized Born-Oppenheimer (LRBO) framework, solving a 5D Hamiltonian numerically using inputs from Density Functional Theory (DFT) and cubic spline interpolation.
model_or_system: The system consists of interstitial hydrogen (H) and deuterium (D) trapped by oxygen impurities in a body-centered-cubic (bcc) Niobium (Nb) lattice, modeled via a 5D subspace including three interstitial modes and two lattice modes (Q and T).
key_observables: Tunnel splitting (J), wavefunction separability metric (epsilon_psi), and lattice-mode probability densities.
important_parameters: Mass regimes (H, D, and mu+), energy asymmetry (delta), and elastic energy cost (Ec).
main_assumptions: The accuracy of the tunnel splitting calculation is approximately 60-70% of experimental values; the derivation assumes the self-trapped H is stable at one site in a fixed lattice.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows a schematic of the tunneling subspace and lattice order parameters; Figure 2 presents potential energy surface and wavefunction contour plots for mu+ and H; Figure 5 displays lattice-mode probability density contours.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of adiabaticity in Nb, describes the 5D LRBO model and DFT methods, presents results comparing H/D to mu+ mass limits, discusses the breakdown of the adiabatic approximation, and concludes with implications for qubit decoherence.
why_interesting: The paper provides a rigorous microscopic foundation for understanding how defect-induced tunneling in solids contributes to decoherence in superconducting qubits, specifically through the modification of the quasiparticle density of states.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Schematic illustration of lattice order parameters defining the tunneling subspace. Nb atoms (green), degener- ate tetrahedral H sites (white circles), and degenerate saddle points (white triangles) and octahedral sites (white squares) are shown within fixed lattice configurations; displaced Nb po- sitions indicate lattice relaxation. Atoms with solid outlines lie in the same plane, while the dashed Nb atom is body-centered. The order parameter Q transforms a singly degenerate hy- drogen configuration (l) into a twofold-degenerate symmetric configuration (s) and subsequently into a second singly degen- erate configuration (r) that is not shown. The order parameter T distorts the...

FIG. 2. Contour plots of the potential energy surface and ground-state wavefunction for two light particle masses: m = mµ+ and m = mH. (a-i) Slices along the qy and T modes with qx = qz = 0, evaluated at three representative lattice configurations: (a, d, g) Q = Ql, (b, e , h) Q = Qs, (c, f, i) Q = Qr. (j-r) Slices along the qx and qy modes with qz = 0 and Q = Qs, shown at three values of the transition-state coordinate: (j, m p) T = T0 = 0, (k, n, q) T = Tst/2, and (l, o, r) T = Tst. Wavefunctions are normalized by their most probable value. Cubic spline interpolation is used to smoothly render the potential energy contours.

Fig. 2a-i present contour plots of the potential energy surface and ground-state wavefunctions of O-trapped H along the qy and T modes for two light-particle masses: m = mµ+ and m = mH, with qx = qz = 0, and at three Q coordinates: Ql, Qs, Qr. As shown in Fig. 2a- c, the primary role of the Q mode is to symmetrize the minima of the double-well potential and reduce the barrier between degenerate hydrogen sites near transition-state configuration T = Tts. For m = mH, the ground state wavefunction remains localized in the two adjacent, self- trapped configurations at Q = Ql and Q = Qr, with suppressed population in the symmetric configuration Q = Qs. FIG. 3. (a) Tunnel splittings (J) as a function of light- particle mass mp, computed using the lattice-renormalized Born-Oppenheimer (LRBO) model and light particle approxi- mation (LPA). (b) Deviation from wavefunction separability, ϵψ = ∥ψλ −ψv pψw n ∥2, of the LRBO (ψλ) and LPA wavefunc- tions (ψpψn), evaluated for ground and first-excited states. The LPA is satisfied only in the µ+ mass limit.

FIG. 4. Adiabatic potential energy surfaces Ev p(Q, T) along the Q coordinate at fixed T. (a) Ground state (E0 p) and first-excited state (E1 p) potential energy surfaces along the Q mode for m = mµ with T set to its value at the local minimum Tm shown in Fig. 2. Well-separated energy levels characteristic of adiabatic behavior occur. (b) Corresponding surfaces for m = mH with T = 0, where only a small avoided crossing (inset) separates E0 p and E1 p near the symmetric configuration.
